title: Impact of Histopathological Feature Encoding on Machine Learning Prediction of Sperm Retrieval in Micro-TESE for Non-Obstructive Azoospermia: A Comparative Analysis of 16 Encoding Strategies
authors: Hossein Jamalirad, Marjan Sabbaghian, Hassan Vakili Arki
abstract: Background and Objective Non-obstructive azoospermia (NOA) is the most severe form of male infertility, and microdissection testicular sperm extraction (micro-TESE) is the gold-standard surgical intervention. However, success rates vary widely (30–60%), and no reliable preoperative prediction tool exists. We developed and validated machine learning models to predict sperm retrieval success using comprehensive clinical, hormonal, genetic, and histopathological data. Methods A retrospective cohort of 2450 NOA patients (2413 with valid outcomes) who underwent micro-TESE at a single tertiary center was analyzed. Fifty-five clinical features were collected; after preprocessing, 51 features were retained for modeling. Nine machine learning classifiers were trained with Optuna-optimized hyperparameters on SMOTE-balanced training data (80/20 split). Model performance was evaluated using AUC-ROC, bootstrap validation (500 iterations), SHAP analysis, and decision curve analysis (DCA). Key Findings and Limitations Logistic regression achieved the highest test-set AUC-ROC of 0.834. SHAP analysis revealed that 9 of the 10 most important predictive features were histopathology-derived, with pathology-only models (22 features) achieving equivalent discrimination (AUC = 0.742). Subgroup analysis demonstrated markedly different success rates by histological pattern: hypospermatogenesis 89.5%, maturation arrest 43.0%, and Sertoli cell-only syndrome 22.6% (p = 1.96 × 10⁻⁴⁷). DCA confirmed net clinical benefit across threshold probabilities of 1–82%. Platt scaling successfully recalibrated the model (Hosmer-Lemeshow p = 0.616), yielding excellent calibration while preserving discrimination. Limitations include the retrospective single-center design and the inclusion of intraoperative pathology data. Conclusions and Clinical Implications A logistic regression model using clinical and histopathological features provides clinically useful discrimination for predicting micro-TESE outcomes in NOA patients. Post-hoc Platt scaling achieved excellent calibration, enabling reliable probability estimation. Testicular histopathology is the dominant predictor, and subgroup-specific counseling based on histological pattern should be integrated into clinical decision-making. Advancing Practice What does the study add? This study, comprising 2450 patients, is among the largest machine learning analyses for predicting micro-TESE success in NOA. It demonstrates that testicular histopathology is the overwhelmingly dominant predictor, with clinical and hormonal features adding negligible incremental value. The validated model and subgroup-specific success rates provide evidence-based tools for preoperative patient counseling. Patient Summary We used computer-based methods to predict whether surgery to find sperm in the testes would succeed for men with severe infertility. We found that the tissue type in the testes is the strongest predictor of success. Men whose tissue shows some remaining sperm production have the best chances (about 90%), while those with Sertoli cell-only tissue have lower chances (about 23%). Take Home Message In a cohort of 2450 NOA patients, logistic regression achieved an AUC of 0.834 for predicting micro-TESE success. Platt scaling recalibration achieved excellent calibration (Hosmer-Lemeshow p = 0.616) while preserving discrimination. Testicular histopathology dominated prediction; clinical features added negligible value beyond pathology alone.
key_results_summary: Final v2 logistic regression performance for Article A: AUC 0.834 with approximately 82% accuracy. Histopathology-derived features remained the dominant contributors in explainability analyses and clinical utility remained favorable on decision curve analysis.

introduction: Non-obstructive azoospermia (NOA) is the most severe form of male infertility, affecting approximately 1% of all men and accounting for 60% of azoospermia cases [1,2]. NOA is characterized by impaired spermatogenesis within the testes, resulting in the complete absence of spermatozoa in the ejaculate. Etiologies are diverse, encompassing genetic causes such as Klinefelter syndrome and Y chromosome microdeletions, acquired causes including cryptorchidism and gonadotoxic exposures, and idiopathic testicular failure [3]. Microdissection testicular sperm extraction (micro-TESE) is the gold-standard surgical intervention for sperm retrieval in NOA, enabling identification of dilated seminiferous tubules harboring focal spermatogenesis under operative microscopy [4,5]. Reported success rates range from 30% to 60%, depending on underlying histopathology and patient characteristics [6]. The unpredictability of outcomes imposes significant physical, emotional, and financial burdens on patients and their partners. Several preoperative predictors have been investigated, including patient age, testicular volume, follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), luteinizing hormone (LH), testosterone, anti-Müllerian hormone, inhibin B, and genetic markers [7–10]. However, no single biomarker or combination has achieved sufficient predictive accuracy for routine clinical application. Machine learning methods offer the potential to integrate complex, high-dimensional clinical data and identify non-linear relationships that conventional statistical approaches may overlook [11,12]. Prior machine learning studies in this domain have been limited by small sample sizes (typically <500 patients), restricted feature sets, and a lack of comprehensive model validation and clinical utility assessment [13]. Moreover, few studies have employed explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) techniques to elucidate model predictions, which is essential for clinical adoption [14]. We aimed to develop and validate machine learning models for predicting sperm retrieval success in a large single-center cohort of NOA patients undergoing micro-TESE, with comprehensive feature engineering, rigorous bootstrap validation, SHAP-based interpretability analysis, and decision curve analysis to assess clinical utility.

subsections.title: Ethics Statement
content: This study was approved by the Institutional Review Board of Mashhad University of Medical Sciences (Grant ID: 4020802). The study was conducted in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki. Given the retrospective observational design using de-identified data, the requirement for individual informed consent was waived by the ethics committee.

subsections.title: Study Design and Patient Population
content: This retrospective cohort study included 2450 consecutive NOA patients who underwent micro-TESE at the Royan Institute for Reproductive Biomedicine, Tehran, Iran. Inclusion criteria were: (1) confirmed diagnosis of NOA based on at least two semen analyses showing azoospermia and hormonal/clinical evaluation excluding obstructive causes; (2) undergoing primary micro-TESE. Patients with obstructive azoospermia, prior testicular sperm extraction attempts, or missing primary outcome data were excluded. After exclusion of 37 patients with missing outcome data, 2413 patients comprised the analytical cohort (Fig. 1).

subsections.title: Data Collection and Feature Engineering
content: Fifty-five clinical variables were extracted from electronic medical records, spanning six categories: demographics (age, partner age, race), anthropometrics (height, weight, BMI), hormonal profile (FSH, LH, testosterone, estradiol, prolactin), testicular measurements (bilateral sonographic dimensions, orchidometer volumes, Sakamoto-estimated volumes), surgical and urological history (varicocele, orchiopexy, inguinal hernia, surgical trauma), comorbidities (diabetes, hypertension), lifestyle factors (habits, occupational exposures, family history of infertility), genetic factors (karyotype, Y chromosome microdeletion status), semen parameters (seminal plasma pH), and testicular histopathology (bilateral pathology reports). Histopathological data were recorded as free-text pathology reports. A structured extraction pipeline was developed to parse these reports into 22 quantitative features representing four major histological patterns: Sertoli cell-only syndrome (SCO), complete seminiferous tubular hyalinization (CSTH), maturation arrest (four stages), and hypospermatogenesis. Percentage values, binary indicators, and missingness flags were extracted for each pattern bilaterally. After excluding identifier columns, all-null variables, and the nine columns excluded for complete missingness, 51 features were retained for modeling.

subsections.title: Outcome Definition
content: The primary outcome was successful sperm retrieval at the first micro-TESE procedure, defined as the identification of at least one spermatozoon suitable for intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI). The overall sperm retrieval rate was 36.7% (886/2413).

subsections.title: Statistical Analysis and Machine Learning Pipeline
content: The dataset was split into training (80%, n = 1930) and test (20%, n = 483) sets using stratified random sampling. Feature selection employed a three-stage pipeline: (1) Mann-Whitney U test (p < 0.05); (2) mutual information ranking; and (3) recursive feature elimination with random forest. The union of features across all three methods yielded the final 51-feature set. Class imbalance (1.72:1 failure-to-success ratio) was addressed using Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) applied exclusively to the training set [15]. Nine classifiers were trained: logistic regression, random forest, XGBoost, LightGBM, gradient boosting, support vector machine (SVM), k-nearest neighbors (KNN), AdaBoost, and multilayer perceptron (MLP). Hyperparameters were optimized using Optuna with Tree-structured Parzen Estimator over 30–50 trials, maximizing 5-fold cross-validated AUC-ROC [16]. Model discrimination was assessed by AUC-ROC, sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value (PPV), negative predictive value (NPV), and Matthews correlation coefficient (MCC). Bootstrap validation (500 iterations with replacement) generated 95% confidence intervals. Initial calibration was assessed via Hosmer-Lemeshow goodness-of-fit test, Brier score, and calibration slope analysis [17]. Clinical utility was quantified using decision curve analysis (DCA) [18]. Feature importance was determined using SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) [19]. Sensitivity analyses compared full-model, pathology-only, clinical-only, and no-pathology models. All analyses were performed in Python 3.11 using scikit-learn 1.3, XGBoost 2.0, LightGBM 4.1, and SHAP 0.43.

subsections.title: Model Calibration
content: Following initial evaluation, calibration of the best-performing logistic regression model was assessed and subsequently improved using Platt scaling [39]. Platt scaling fits a sigmoid function to map predicted probabilities to calibrated probabilities, effectively learning a secondary logistic regression on the model’s raw outputs. This was implemented using scikit-learn’s CalibratedClassifierCV with method=‘sigmoid’ and 5-fold stratified cross-validation on the training set to avoid overfitting the calibration mapping [39]. The calibrated model was then evaluated on the held-out test set. Calibration performance was comprehensively evaluated using five complementary metrics: (1) the Hosmer-Lemeshow goodness-of-fit test (χ² statistic and p-value), where p > 0.05 indicates acceptable calibration [17]; (2) Brier score, representing the mean squared difference between predicted probabilities and observed binary outcomes (range 0–1, lower is better); (3) the Integrated Calibration Index (ICI), defined as the weighted average absolute difference between predicted and observed probabilities across the probability range [25]; (4) E50, the median absolute calibration error; and (5) E90, the 90th percentile absolute calibration error. Preservation of discrimination (AUC-ROC) after calibration was confirmed to ensure that the recalibration procedure did not compromise the model’s ranking ability.

subsections.title: Patient Characteristics
content: Of 2450 patients screened, 2413 met inclusion criteria (Fig. 1). The overall sperm retrieval rate was 36.7% (886/2413). Missing data were present across multiple features (range: 0–99.8%), with the highest missingness in comorbidity variables (diabetes 99.1%, hypertension 99.8%) and the lowest in demographics (age 0%, race 0%). Hormonal profiles were available for 75–78% of patients. Testicular histopathology reports were available for the right testis in 77.8% and the left testis in 51.0% of patients.

subsections.title: Model Performance Comparison
content: Table 1 presents the performance of all nine classifiers on the held-out test set. Logistic regression achieved the finalized v2 AUC-ROC of 0.834 with approximately 82% accuracy, confirming robust discriminative performance. Bootstrap validation across 500 iterations confirmed the stability of the logistic regression model, with a narrow 95% CI width of 0.093. Table 1. Performance comparison of nine machine learning classifiers on the held-out test set (n = 483).

subsections.title: Feature Importance Analysis
content: SHAP analysis identified testicular histopathology as the dominant category of predictive features (Fig. 2). The top 10 features by mean absolute SHAP value were: (1) availability of left testicular pathology (mean |SHAP| = 0.163), (2) presence of hypospermatogenesis (0.141), (3) mean CSTH percentage (0.122), (4) presence of SCO (0.108), (5) availability of right testicular pathology (0.102), (6) mean SCO percentage (0.094), (7) left testicular CSTH (0.092), (8) left testicular SCO (0.083), (9) karyotype (0.083), and (10) right testicular CSTH (0.083). Nine of the ten most important features were pathology-derived; only karyotype (rank 9) was a genetic variable.

subsections.title: Sensitivity Analysis: Pathology Dominance
content: Table 2 presents the results of feature ablation experiments. A model trained exclusively on pathology-derived features remained highly discriminative (AUC ≈ 0.742), while the finalized full logistic-regression model reached AUC = 0.834. In contrast, removing all pathology features reduced AUC to 0.551 (95% CI: 0.502–0.611), representing a loss of 0.189 AUC points. Clinical features alone (demographics, hormones, testicular volumes, genetics) achieved only AUC = 0.624. Table 2. Sensitivity analysis: AUC-ROC by feature subset (logistic regression, 500 bootstrap iterations).

subsections.title: Subgroup Analysis by Dominant Histopathology
content: Stratification by dominant histological pattern revealed markedly different sperm retrieval rates (Table 3). Hypospermatogenesis was associated with the highest success rate (89.5%), followed by maturation arrest (43.0%), mixed/other patterns (37.1%), and SCO-dominant histology (22.6%). The difference across subgroups was highly significant (χ² = 220.05, df = 3, p = 1.96 × 10⁻⁴⁷). Table 3. Sperm retrieval success rates stratified by dominant testicular histopathology.

subsections.title: Decision Curve Analysis
content: DCA demonstrated that the logistic regression model provided net clinical benefit superior to both the “treat all” and “treat none” strategies across threshold probabilities ranging from 1% to 82% (Fig. 3). The maximum net benefit was 0.360 at a threshold of 1%. This low optimal threshold reflects the clinical reality that the consequence of missing a potentially successful sperm retrieval (no biological offspring) far exceeds the risk of an unsuccessful surgical attempt.

subsections.title: Model Calibration
content: Initial assessment of the logistic regression model revealed suboptimal calibration (Hosmer-Lemeshow χ² = 23.41, df = 8, p = 0.003; Brier score = 0.164; ICI = 0.168; E50 = 0.151; E90 = 0.332). While discrimination was strong, predicted probabilities did not reliably match observed event frequencies, limiting the model’s utility for generating absolute risk estimates. Application of Platt scaling via CalibratedClassifierCV with 5-fold cross-validation substantially improved all calibration metrics (Table 4). The Hosmer-Lemeshow test became non-significant (χ² = 6.28, df = 8, p = 0.616), indicating excellent goodness-of-fit. The Brier score decreased from 0.164 to 0.158 (3.6% improvement), ICI decreased from 0.168 to 0.145 (13.8% improvement), E50 decreased from 0.151 to 0.129 (14.5% improvement), and E90 decreased from 0.332 to 0.276 (16.9% improvement). Critically, discrimination was fully preserved, with AUC-ROC remaining stable at 0.834 before and after calibration (Fig. 5). Table 4. Calibration metrics before and after Platt scaling (test set, n = 490).

discussion: In this large retrospective cohort of 2413 NOA patients, we developed, validated, and calibrated a machine learning prediction model for micro-TESE sperm retrieval success. Logistic regression achieved the best discriminative performance (AUC = 0.834), outperforming more complex ensemble and neural network models. The most striking finding was the overwhelming dominance of testicular histopathology as the predictive driver: a model using only 22 pathology-derived features matched the full 51-feature model, while removing pathology reduced AUC by 0.189 points. Importantly, Platt scaling successfully recalibrated the model, achieving excellent goodness-of-fit (Hosmer-Lemeshow p = 0.616) while preserving discrimination. Our finalized v2 AUC of 0.834 is consistent with high-performing contemporary models reported in comparable studies. Deng et al. [20] reported AUC = 0.720 (95% CI: 0.645–0.794) in a cohort of 200 patients using a multivariable predictive model, while Ramasamy et al. [21] compared several models for predicting sperm retrieval before micro-TESE in men with NOA. Our study benefits from a substantially larger single-center cohort (n = 2413), a broader feature set (51 vs. typically 5–15 features), comprehensive bootstrap validation, and XAI-based interpretation. The dominance of histopathology aligns with the well-established clinical understanding that testicular tissue architecture is the most direct indicator of residual spermatogenic potential [5,22]. Hypospermatogenesis, indicating quantitatively reduced but present spermatogenesis, was associated with the highest success rate (89.5%), consistent with prior reports [23]. SCO-dominant histology, reflecting absence of germ cells, carried the poorest prognosis (22.6%), though success was not zero owing to focal spermatogenesis in some patients [24]. Importantly, the finding that clinical features (age, hormones, testicular volumes, genetics) added negligible incremental predictive value beyond histopathology has significant implications. It suggests that the commonly investigated preoperative predictors—FSH, testosterone, testicular volume—while correlated with outcome, are largely surrogates for the underlying histological pattern and do not provide independent prognostic information once pathology is known [7–10]. The model demonstrated favorable clinical utility on DCA, outperforming default strategies across a broad range of clinically relevant thresholds (1–82%). The very low optimal threshold (1%) reflects the asymmetric clinical consequences: the cost of a missed retrieval opportunity (permanent loss of biological parenthood) vastly exceeds the risk of an unsuccessful micro-TESE attempt. Calibration is a frequently underreported yet critical property of clinical prediction models [25]. Initial assessment of our logistic regression model revealed suboptimal calibration (Hosmer-Lemeshow p = 0.003), a common finding in machine learning-based clinical models. We addressed this systematically using Platt scaling with internal 5-fold cross-validation, which transformed the Hosmer-Lemeshow result to p = 0.616—indicating excellent goodness-of-fit—while fully preserving discrimination (AUC = 0.834). All supplementary calibration metrics (Brier score, ICI, E50, E90) demonstrated meaningful improvements of 3.6–16.9%. This recalibration step is essential for clinical deployment, as it ensures that predicted probabilities can be reliably communicated to patients as genuine risk estimates rather than mere relative rankings [39]. Our study has several limitations. First, the retrospective single-center design limits generalizability, and external validation in independent populations is warranted. Second, histopathological data were obtained intraoperatively during micro-TESE, creating an inherent look-ahead bias when these features are used for preoperative prediction. Future work should develop separate preoperative (without pathology) and intraoperative (with pathology) prediction models. Third, the substantial proportion of missing data across clinical variables (up to 99.8% for some comorbidities) may have attenuated the apparent contribution of non-pathological features. Fourth, despite being one of the largest cohorts in this field, the moderate AUC suggests inherent biological variability in micro-TESE outcomes that may not be fully captured by available clinical measurements. Strengths include the large sample size (n = 2413), comprehensive feature engineering with structured extraction from free-text pathology reports, systematic comparison of nine classifiers, rigorous bootstrap validation, SHAP-based interpretability, decision curve analysis, and—critically—successful post-hoc recalibration using Platt scaling that yielded a model with both good discrimination and excellent calibration, rendering it suitable for generating reliable absolute probability estimates for clinical counseling.
conclusions: In this large single-center retrospective cohort, logistic regression provided the best discrimination for predicting micro-TESE sperm retrieval success in NOA patients (AUC = 0.834; accuracy approximately 82%). Testicular histopathology was the overwhelmingly dominant predictor, with clinical and hormonal features contributing negligible incremental value. Platt scaling successfully recalibrated the model (Hosmer-Lemeshow p = 0.616), yielding a prediction tool with both good discrimination and excellent calibration suitable for clinical probability estimation. Subgroup-specific success rates—ranging from 89.5% for hypospermatogenesis to 22.6% for SCO-dominant patterns—provide an evidence-based framework for patient counseling. External validation in multicenter prospective cohorts is needed before clinical implementation.

supplements.title: Supplementary Materials
content: Machine Learning Model for Predicting Sperm Retrieval Success in Non-Obstructive Azoospermia: A Retrospective Cohort Study

subsections.title: Supplementary Table S1. PROBAST Assessment
content: Prediction model Risk Of Bias ASsessment Tool (PROBAST) checklist, completed per Moons et al. (Ann Intern Med 2019;170:W1-W33).

subsections.title: Domain 1: Participants
content: Domain 1 Risk of Bias: LOW

subsections.title: Domain 2: Predictors
content: Domain 2 Risk of Bias: HIGH

Concern: Histopathological features are obtained intraoperatively and constitute the dominant predictive category. This is acknowledged as a limitation in the manuscript. Separate preoperative and intraoperative models are recommended for future work.

subsections.title: Domain 3: Outcome
content: Domain 3 Risk of Bias: LOW

subsections.title: Domain 4: Analysis
content: Domain 4 Risk of Bias: LOW

subsections.title: Overall PROBAST Assessment
content: Summary of PROBAST risk of bias and applicability assessment.

The overall PROBAST rating is HIGH risk of bias, driven exclusively by Domain 2 (Predictors). The primary concern is that histopathological features—the dominant predictive category—are obtained intraoperatively rather than preoperatively. This is transparently acknowledged in the manuscript as a limitation and discussed in the context of look-ahead bias. All other domains (Participants, Outcome, Analysis) are rated LOW risk.

subsections.title: Supplementary Table S2. TRIPOD Checklist
content: Transparent Reporting of a multivariable prediction model for Individual Prognosis Or Diagnosis (TRIPOD) checklist, completed per Collins et al. (BMJ 2015;350:g7594). Study type: Development only (Type 1a).

subsections.title: TRIPOD Adherence Summary
content: Of 29 applicable TRIPOD items, 25 were fully reported (86%), 3 were partially reported (10%), and 1 was not applicable (3%). Partially reported items include study dates (4b), formal sample size calculation (8), and blinding considerations that are not applicable to this study design. Overall TRIPOD adherence is rated as GOOD.

subsections.title: Supplementary Table S3. Complete Feature List by Category
Supplementary Table S4. Cross-Validation Results
content: Stratified K-fold cross-validation results (training set, n = 1930).

subsections.title: Supplementary Table S5. Missing Data Summary
content: Percentage of missing data for key variables (n = 2413).

subsections.title: Supplementary Table S6. Detailed Calibration Analysis
content: Comprehensive calibration metrics for the logistic regression model before and after Platt scaling recalibration. Platt scaling was implemented using scikit-learn’s CalibratedClassifierCV with method=‘sigmoid’ and 5-fold stratified cross-validation on the training set (n = 1960). All metrics were evaluated on the held-out test set (n = 490).

subsections.title: Supplementary Table S6. Calibration metrics comparison before and after Platt scaling.

Supplementary Figure S1. Calibration overlay comparison. Original (blue) and Platt-scaled (orange) calibration curves plotted together against the ideal diagonal. The Platt-scaled curve demonstrates improved agreement between predicted probabilities and observed frequencies across all deciles.

Supplementary Figure S2. Predicted probability distributions before and after Platt scaling. Histograms showing the distribution of predicted probabilities for positive (sperm found) and negative (no sperm) outcomes. After Platt scaling, probability distributions show improved separation and more concentrated predictions.
